summary: My grandfather arrived in San Francisco in January 1850 after sailing around the Horn from Kiel. He initially tried his luck in the gold mines but soon returned to San Francisco to run lodging houses and hotels, including the New Orleans Hotel on Post Street. I was born in San Francisco in 1893 and spent my early childhood in the North Beach area, attending school there before moving to Sonoma.

Kiel was my grandfather's hometown in Schleswig-Holstein, where his father was a cabinet-maker. He left Kiel in 1849 because he felt oppressed under Danish rule and sought a better future in California. It was from Kiel that he embarked on his journey to San Francisco during the Gold Rush.

Schleswig-Holstein was the state where my grandfather lived before emigrating. At that time, it was under Danish government, which he felt was oppressive to the German population. This political situation motivated him to leave in 1849 and seek opportunity in California during the Gold Rush.

Denmark governed Schleswig-Holstein when my grandfather lived there, and he felt oppressed by this Danish rule. This was one of the main reasons he decided to leave his home in 1849 and head to California. The Danish domination shaped his view of his future prospects in Europe.

The California Gold Rush was the event that inspired my grandfather to leave Kiel in 1849. He believed California was the land of opportunity and sailed around the Horn to reach San Francisco in 1850. Although he tried mining, he soon realized it was too difficult and instead turned to running lodging houses and hotels in San Francisco.

My grandfather had a brother who left Kiel at the same time as him but went to South America instead of California. That brother joined the forces of a South American liberator, and unfortunately, they never heard from each other again. So South America was part of my family’s story, though indirectly.

John C. Lutgens was my grandfather’s name. He came from Kiel in 1850 and initially tried mining but soon became a lodging-house keeper in San Francisco by 1852 or 1853. Later he ran the New Orleans Hotel on Post Street and eventually built the Union Hotel in Sonoma. He was also secretary of the Franklin Hospital in San Francisco before he passed away in 1904.

Wells Fargo is where I found old San Francisco directories that listed my grandfather, John C. Lutgens, as a lodging-house keeper in 1852 or 1853. Their library helped me trace his early business activities in the city after he returned from the mines. It was an important source for understanding his role in San Francisco’s hotel scene.

My grandfather ran the New Orleans Hotel, which was located on Post Street between Kearny and Grant in San Francisco. This was after he gave up mining and decided to go into the hotel business. It was during this time he brought Johanna Niepah from Hamburg to marry her in San Francisco.

Post Street in San Francisco was where my grandfather’s New Orleans Hotel was located, specifically between Kearny and Grant streets. This area was central to his hotel business in the early 1850s after he returned from the mines. It was a key location in his life and work in the city.

Kearny Street was one of the streets bordering the location of my grandfather’s New Orleans Hotel in San Francisco. The hotel stood on Post Street between Kearny and Grant, making Kearny a familiar place in his hotel operations during the early 1850s. It was part of the bustling city center where he worked.

Grant Avenue was near the site of my grandfather’s New Orleans Hotel on Post Street in San Francisco. It was one of the streets that defined the hotel’s location in the early 1850s. Later, my grandfather’s family also had connections to Grant Avenue through my other grandfather’s bakery work.

Hamburg was the city my grandfather sent to when he brought Johanna Niepah to San Francisco to marry her. They met after he left Kiel, but I don’t know much about her family. Hamburg was important as the place where Johanna came from before joining my grandfather in California.

Johanna Niepah was the woman my grandfather met after leaving Kiel and later brought from Hamburg to San Francisco to marry. Sadly, she died early in their life together, during childbirth with their seventh child. She was an important part of my grandfather’s family story in California.

My grandfather built and ran the old Union Hotel in Sonoma, California after his time in San Francisco. The hotel was eventually razed and replaced by a Bank of America branch, but the cornerstone of the old building was found during that process. The Union Hotel was a significant part of his hotel business legacy outside the city.

Bank of America now stands on the spot where my grandfather’s old Union Hotel in Sonoma once was. When the hotel was razed, they found the cornerstone of the original building. This shows how the place where my family once worked has changed over time but still holds historical significance.

Sonoma was where my grandfather built the Union Hotel after his years in San Francisco. My father was actually born there, and later we moved back to Sonoma where my family had a small ranch. It was an important place in our family’s history and where we spent part of my childhood.

After running the Union Hotel in Sonoma, my grandfather moved to Ukiah, California, where he continued in the hotel business. This was another chapter in his career before he returned to San Francisco to work with the Franklin Hospital. Ukiah was part of his journey through northern California.

Later in life, my grandfather became the secretary of Franklin Hospital in San Francisco, which was known then as the German hospital. He was in charge of the hospital’s plans and operations during that time. This role marked a shift from his hotel business to healthcare administration before he passed away in 1904.

My mother’s family came from southern Germany, where my other grandfather Backer was a baker. He worked in San Francisco at the Winkle bakery on Sutter Street. So Germany was the origin of my maternal family and influenced our family’s trades and lives in California.

The Winkle bakery on Sutter Street in San Francisco was where my maternal grandfather Backer worked as a baker. He met and married a daughter of the Winkle family there, and my mother was born from that marriage. The bakery was a key part of my mother’s family history in the city.

Sutter Street in San Francisco was the location of the Winkle bakery where my maternal grandfather worked. It was above Grant Avenue and part of the neighborhood where my mother’s family lived and worked. This street connects to my family’s German baking heritage in the city.

North Beach was where my family lived during my early childhood in San Francisco, specifically near Filbert and Leavenworth streets. I attended kindergarten and primary school in the city before we moved to Sonoma. North Beach was the neighborhood of my earliest memories.

Filbert Street in North Beach, San Francisco, was part of the neighborhood where my family lived when I was a child. We lived near the corner of Filbert and Leavenworth streets during those early years before moving to Sonoma. It was a familiar place for me growing up.

Leavenworth Street intersected with Filbert Street in North Beach, where my family lived during my early years in San Francisco. This neighborhood was where I attended kindergarten and primary school before we relocated to Sonoma. Leavenworth Street was part of my childhood environment.

My father worked for a hardware concern called Miller, Sloss, and Scott in San Francisco while we lived in North Beach. This was during my early childhood before we moved to Sonoma. His work there was part of our family’s livelihood in the city.

I attended kindergarten on Greenwich Street in San Francisco before going to the Sarah B. Cooper primary school. This was during the time my family lived in the city, before moving to Sonoma. Greenwich Street was part of my early education experience.

The Sarah B. Cooper primary school was where I went to school after kindergarten in San Francisco. It was part of my early schooling before my family moved to Sonoma. This school represents my first formal education in the city.
